source.attribution: Written for Bardo OS — the instrument describing itself as built.

blocks.en: The Texts button at the top left opens the catalogue of the whole cycle, arranged as the tradition arranges it: the six bardos in order, each with its instructions and pointing-out texts, followed by the prayers, the supporting texts, and this Guide. Tap a shelf heading to fold or unfold it; tap a title to open the text. Titles whose translation is still forthcoming are listed but locked — the shape of the cycle is shown honestly, including what is not here yet.

Each text is presented passage by passage in up to three parallel layers: Tibetan script, phonetics, and English. The TIB, PHO, and ENG buttons in the bottom bar switch each layer on or off — ENG alone for reading aloud in English, all three for study. Phonetics belong chiefly to liturgy that is meant to be chanted; many passages have none.

The passages differ in what you are meant to do with them. Passages marked ◆ READ ALOUD — shaded, with a gold rule down the left — are spoken directly to the dying or deceased person: read them slowly and plainly, inserting the person's name where the text indicates. Framed panels marked BARDO RECITATION are verses for the deceased to take up and hold. Passages marked LITURGY are recited by the living. Quiet, centered passages like this one are guidance for you, the reader — when to speak, what to prepare, which prayer follows — and are never read aloud. Titles, colophons, and attributions are silent apparatus.
When an instruction mentions a prayer that lives elsewhere in the cycle, a link appears beneath the passage; tapping it opens that prayer directly.

The diamond button switches to Voice mode — the mode you use when someone is dying. The header, the catalogue, and the language toggles disappear; only what is spoken remains, set slightly larger. Guidance passages collapse into thin markers on the page — tap one to peek at it, tap it again to close. Everything else steps out of the way. Press the diamond again to return to Guide mode.

The play button starts a slow, steady scroll, so that both hands stay free. The − and + buttons adjust its pace in ten steps. Touching the page pauses the scroll instantly; after a moment of stillness it resumes on its own, without jumping. When a repeated formula arrives, the scroll rests on it briefly before moving on.

The sun and moon button switches between day and night; until you choose, the app follows the device. The magnifier buttons adjust the text size. The frame button enters fullscreen, and the header withdraws. All of these choices are remembered on the device. The globe at the top right selects the interface language — it governs the app's own labels only and never touches the texts; Tibetan and Polish interfaces will appear there when their translations arrive.

Bardo OS is an installable web app. Once it has opened on your device, the entire corpus is stored locally and everything works without a connection — add it to your home screen and it behaves like any other app. This matters where the app is most needed: at a bedside, where connectivity cannot be assumed. Nothing here requires an account, and nothing is sent anywhere.
